---
title: "Get started"
---

## Two install paths

ferx has two builds. They produce the **same parameter estimates** — they differ in how gradients are computed and how fast fits run.

| Build | Gradients | Compile time | Fit speed | When to use |
|-------|-----------|--------------|-----------|-------------|
| **Simple** (default) | Finite differences | ~2 min | Baseline | Trying ferx out, Windows-native, no Rust nightly |
| **Full** (Enzyme autodiff) | Exact via [Enzyme](https://enzyme.mit.edu/) | ~45–60 min one-time toolchain build | Faster, more accurate Hessians | Production fits, large models, SIR / uncertainty quantification |

You can start with the simple build and upgrade later — the R API is identical.

For a deeper walkthrough of either path, see the installation chapter of the ferx-r book.

### What you give up with the simple build

Almost nothing is *disabled* — the simple build falls back to finite-difference (FD) gradients everywhere autodiff would be used:

| Feature | Simple build |
|---|---|
| `ferx_simulate()`, `ferx_predict()` | Identical — no gradients used |
| ODE / SDE / EKF models | Identical — these always use FD, even in the full build |
| SAEM | Identical — derivative-free E-step |
| Population (outer-loop) optimizer | Identical — always FD in both builds |
| FOCE / FOCEI inner loop with analytical 1-/2-cpt PK | **Slower** — FD instead of Enzyme reverse-mode AD (≈ 2 × n_eta extra gradient calls per iteration); results match |
| Hessian-based standard errors | Slightly less accurate (FD Hessian) |

If you mostly fit ODE models or use SAEM, the full build buys you nothing. The full build's main win is faster FOCEI on analytical-PK models and crisper SEs for SIR / uncertainty quantification work. Tracked in [ferx-r#46](https://github.com/FeRx-NLME/ferx-r/issues/46).

---

## Simple build

The simple build skips the Enzyme toolchain entirely. It works on plain stable Rust and falls back to finite-difference gradients. Estimates match the full build; fits are somewhat slower and standard errors from the FD Hessian are slightly less accurate.

**Prerequisites:** R ≥ 4.2, cmake, and Rust stable (via [rustup](https://rustup.rs)).

### macOS

```bash
brew install cmake
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh
```

RStudio on macOS does not inherit the Homebrew `PATH`. Add cmake explicitly before installing:

```r
# Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3):
Sys.setenv(PATH = paste("/opt/homebrew/opt/cmake/bin", Sys.getenv("PATH"), sep = ":"))
# Intel Mac:
# Sys.setenv(PATH = paste("/usr/local/opt/cmake/bin", Sys.getenv("PATH"), sep = ":"))

Sys.setenv(FERX_NO_AUTODIFF = "1")
pak::pak("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")
```

### Windows

Install [Rtools44](https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/Rtools/rtools44/rtools.html) and [Rust via rustup](https://rustup.rs) (cmake ships with Rtools44), then in R:

```r
Sys.setenv(FERX_NO_AUTODIFF = "1")
pak::pak("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")
```

### Linux

```bash
sudo apt-get install cmake   # Debian / Ubuntu
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh
```

```r
Sys.setenv(FERX_NO_AUTODIFF = "1")
pak::pak("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")
```

The `FERX_NO_AUTODIFF=1` flag tells the package build to skip the Enzyme probe and go straight to the stable, finite-difference path.

---

## Full build (Enzyme autodiff)

The full build uses [Enzyme](https://enzyme.mit.edu/) to compute exact gradients via automatic differentiation. This requires building a custom `rustc` from source with Enzyme integrated into its own LLVM — a one-time ~45–60 min setup that needs ~30 GB of free disk.

::: {.callout-important}
**Do not** build a standalone Enzyme *plugin* against a separately-installed LLVM and copy it into a prebuilt nightly's sysroot. That setup looks like it works but causes Enzyme to **hang silently** in `llvm::Constant::getNullValue` the first time it actually differentiates anything. The full toolchain must be built from a single `rust-lang/rust` source tree with `--enable-llvm-enzyme` and `--set llvm.download-ci-llvm=false`. See the warning at the top of the [ferx-core installation page](https://ferx-nlme.github.io/ferx-core/installation.html#do-not-build-a-standalone-enzyme-plugin-against-a-separately-installed-llvm).
:::

### 1. Build the Enzyme toolchain

Follow the platform steps in the ferx-core book:

- [**Linux**](https://ferx-nlme.github.io/ferx-core/installation.html#linux)
- [**macOS**](https://ferx-nlme.github.io/ferx-core/installation.html#macos) (Intel and Apple Silicon)
- [**Windows**](https://ferx-nlme.github.io/ferx-core/installation.html#windows) — not supported; use WSL2 or Docker

Then [verify](https://ferx-nlme.github.io/ferx-core/installation.html#4-verify-the-toolchain) the toolchain with a real `#[autodiff_forward]` test — the usual `E0601` check is **not** sufficient and passes for broken toolchains.

For shared servers, see [multi-user installs](https://ferx-nlme.github.io/ferx-core/installation.html#multi-user-servers).

### 2. Install the R package

Once `rustc +enzyme` is verified working:

```r
pak::pak("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")
```

The package's `src/Makevars` auto-detects the `enzyme` toolchain and builds the autodiff path. To confirm afterwards:

```r
ferx:::ferx_rust_autodiff_enabled()
# [1] TRUE
```

To force one path or the other, set before installing:

- `FERX_NO_AUTODIFF=1` — force the simple build even if Enzyme is installed
- `FERX_NO_AUTODIFF=0` — force the full build, erroring out if Enzyme isn't found

---

## Docker (full autodiff, no local toolchain)

The [ferx-r Docker image](https://github.com/FeRx-NLME/ferx-r) ships the Enzyme toolchain prebuilt — the fastest way to get a working full-autodiff setup, especially on Windows.

```bash
docker build -t ferx:latest https://github.com/FeRx-NLME/ferx-r.git#main
docker run --rm -p 8787:8787 -e PASSWORD=ferx ferx:latest
```

Open [http://localhost:8787](http://localhost:8787) — user: `rstudio`, password: `ferx`.

---

## First fit

After either install, run the bundled warfarin example:

```r
library(ferx)
ex  <- ferx_example("warfarin")
fit <- ferx_fit(ex$model, ex$data)
print(fit)
summary(fit)
```

List all bundled examples:

```r
ferx_example()   # character vector of available example names
?ferx_example    # full list with descriptions
```

---

## Next steps

- [ferx-r](https://ferx-nlme.github.io/ferx-book/) — narrative guide to the R package with runnable examples
- [ferx-core](https://ferx-nlme.github.io/ferx-core/) — mdBook for the Rust engine, CLI, and `.ferx` DSL spec
- [Model workflow](learn/model-workflow.qmd) — full fitting workflow
- [Model DSL](model-dsl/overview.qmd) — write your own `.ferx` model files
- [Reference](reference/index.html) — function documentation
- [ferx-core installation](https://ferx-nlme.github.io/ferx-core/installation.html) — full toolchain detail and troubleshooting
